schools.summary: Ruth's appeal that Boaz spread his skirt over her, for he was a near kinsman: 'and he said, Who art thou? And she answered, I am Ruth thine handmaid: spread therefore thy skirt over thine handmaid; for thou art a near kinsman' — the claim of the redeemer's covenant — 'and she answered, I am Ruth thine handmaid: spread therefore thy skirt over thine handmaid; for thou art a near kinsman': Ruth, owning herself his handmaid, appeals to Boaz to take her under his protection by marriage, as the near kinsman (the witnesses noting the significance — the modest yet bold appeal of Ruth, who, claiming the right of the kinsman-redeemer (the goel), asks Boaz to spread his skirt over her, the well-known token of taking a woman under one's protection and into marriage, the very phrase by which God himself describes his covenant taking of Israel, so that Ruth's appeal is a claim upon the redeemer's covenant duty and a casting of herself wholly upon his grace and faithfulness, the spreading of the skirt the sign of espousal and protection, the near kinsman the goel bound by the law to redeem, the suppliant claiming not merely pity but covenant right, the lesson — that the believer comes to the Redeemer claiming the covenant relation and casting herself on his faithfulness, 'spread thy skirt over thine handmaid,' as God says, 'I spread my skirt over thee... and thou becamest mine,' Ezekiel 16:8, the truth that the kinsman-redeemer is bound to redeem his own, and the suppliant may plead that bond, the right of the goel that points to the great Redeemer of his people, 'thy Maker is thine husband; the LORD of hosts is his name... thy Redeemer,' Isaiah 54:5, the figure — the soul that comes to Christ the near Kinsman and Redeemer, claiming his covenant protection and resting under the shadow of his wings, fulfilled in him who took our nature to redeem us, 'forasmuch then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, he also himself likewise took part of the same,' Hebrews 2:14, the truth that to be redeemed we must come to the Redeemer and claim him as our own, the figure of the suppliant who claims the redeemer's covenant protection by right of kinship, the call to cast ourselves on Christ the near Kinsman, the assurance that the Redeemer spreads his covenant over those who claim him); Ruth answering modestly, owning herself his handmaid, presenting herself to the kinsman (the handmaid before the redeemer, Ezekiel 16:8) — the answer (I am Ruth thine handmaid); 'spread therefore thy skirt over thine handmaid; for thou art a near kinsman' (Ruth asking Boaz to spread his skirt over her, the token of protection and espousal, claiming the right of the kinsman-redeemer bound by the law, casting herself on his covenant faithfulness, the goel that points to the great Redeemer, the claim of the redeemer's covenant, Isaiah 54:5; Hebrews 2:14) — the figure (the soul that comes to Christ the near Kinsman, claiming his covenant protection, the providence, the appeal, Boaz blessing Ruth and undertaking the matter to follow, vv.10-13) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Ruth's appeal 'spread thy skirt over thine handmaid' signify?

sides.position: The suppliant who claims the redeemer's covenant protection by right of kinship - Ruth claiming the right of the kinsman-redeemer, asking the token of protection and marriage, the very phrase of God's covenant taking of Israel (Ezekiel 16:8), the goel bound to redeem his own and pointing to the great Redeemer (Isaiah 54:5), the soul that comes to Christ the near Kinsman who took our nature to redeem us (Hebrews 2:14)

schools.summary: Boaz blessing Ruth for her greater kindness in seeking the kinsman: 'and he said, Blessed be thou of the LORD, my daughter: for thou hast shewed more kindness in the latter end than at the beginning, inasmuch as thou followedst not young men, whether poor or rich' — Boaz blessed Ruth's kindness in seeking the kinsman rather than youth or wealth (the and he said, Blessed be thou of the LORD, my daughter, the blessing, the for thou hast shewed more kindness in the latter end than at the beginning, inasmuch as thou followedst not young men, whether poor or rich, the greater kindness, the figure, the kindness commended that sought the kinsman's duty rather than self, the truth that Boaz praised Ruth's kindness in seeking the redeemer for Naomi's sake, Ruth 2:11, the figure of the kindness that seeks duty and the good of others above its own desire, the blessing of Boaz, the kindness commended, vv.2:11); Boaz blessing Ruth (the commendation, Ruth 2:11) — the blessing (blessed be thou); 'thou hast shewed more kindness in the latter end... inasmuch as thou followedst not young men' (Ruth's kindness in seeking the kinsman, the virtue, the figure, the kindness that seeks duty and the good of others above its own desire, the providence, the blessing, Boaz's promise to do all she asked to follow, vv.11) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Boaz's solemn vow, sworn by the living LORD, to do the kinsman's part of redemption if the nearer kinsman would not: 'tarry this night, and it shall be in the morning, that if he will perform unto thee the part of a kinsman, well; let him do the kinsman's part: but if he will not do the part of a kinsman to thee, then will I do the part of a kinsman to thee, as the LORD liveth: lie down until the morning' — the sworn pledge of the redeemer — 'if he will perform unto thee the part of a kinsman, well... but if he will not... then will I do the part of a kinsman to thee, as the LORD liveth': Boaz binds himself by a solemn oath to redeem Ruth if the nearer kinsman declines (the witnesses noting the significance — the firm and sworn engagement of Boaz, who, having owned the prior right of the nearer kinsman, binds himself with an oath in the name of the living God that, should the other decline, he himself will surely perform the kinsman's part of redemption, so that Ruth has the redeemer's own oath as the pledge and security of her redemption, her cause now resting not on her own claim but on his sworn faithfulness, the oath 'as the LORD liveth' the surest of bonds, the redeemer's word made doubly sure, the suppliant's redemption guaranteed by the redeemer's vow, the lesson — that the believer's redemption rests on the sure word and oath of the Redeemer, who binds himself to redeem his own, 'wherein God, willing more abundantly to shew unto the heirs of promise the immutability of his counsel, confirmed it by an oath,' Hebrews 6:17, the truth that the Redeemer's pledge is the ground of the believer's assurance, his sworn faithfulness more than all our hopes, 'faithful is he that calleth you, who also will do it,' 1 Thessalonians 5:24, the figure — the great Redeemer who has bound himself by oath and covenant to redeem his people, so that their salvation rests on his unchangeable word, fulfilled in him whose oath secures the covenant, 'the Lord sware and will not repent, Thou art a priest for ever,' Hebrews 7:21, the truth that the redeemed may rest in the sworn surety of the Redeemer, the figure of the redeemer's sure and sworn pledge to redeem his own, the call to rest on the oath and faithfulness of Christ our Redeemer, the assurance that he who has sworn to redeem will surely perform it); Boaz first giving the nearer kinsman his right, leaving him the first opportunity to redeem, the right order honoured (the prior claim, Hebrews 6:17) — the order (let him do the kinsman's part); 'but if he will not do the part of a kinsman to thee, then will I do the part of a kinsman to thee, as the LORD liveth: lie down until the morning' (Boaz binding himself by oath in the name of the living God to redeem Ruth if the other declined, the redeemer's word made sure, Ruth's redemption guaranteed by his sworn faithfulness, the sworn pledge of the redeemer, 1 Thessalonians 5:24; Hebrews 7:21) — the figure (the great Redeemer who has bound himself by oath to redeem his people, the providence, the vow, Ruth lying down and Boaz settling it at the gate to follow, vv.14-18; ch.4) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Boaz's oath 'as the LORD liveth' to redeem signify?

sides.position: The redeemer's sure and sworn pledge to redeem his own - Boaz binding himself with an oath in the name of the living God that he will surely redeem, so Ruth's redemption rests on his sworn faithfulness, the believer's redemption resting on the oath of the Redeemer who binds himself to redeem his own (Hebrews 6:17), the Redeemer's pledge the ground of assurance (1 Thessalonians 5:24), the great Redeemer bound by oath and covenant (Hebrews 7:21)

themes: Tarry this night, and it shall be in the morning, that if he will perform unto thee the part of a kinsman, well; let him do the kinsman's part: but if he will not do the part of a kinsman to thee, then will I do the part of a kinsman to thee, as the LORD liveth: lie down until the morning.

Boaz sending the barley so Naomi, who had come home empty, should not remain so
the redeemer who fills the empty and cares for the desolate (Ruth 1:21)

schools.summary: Naomi bidding Ruth wait, for Boaz would not rest till the matter was finished: 'then said she, Sit still, my daughter, until thou know how the matter will fall: for the man will not be in rest, until he have finished the thing this day' — Naomi assured Ruth that Boaz would settle the matter that very day (the then said she, Sit still, my daughter, until thou know how the matter will fall, the waiting, the for the man will not be in rest, until he have finished the thing this day, the redeemer's diligence, the figure, the confidence in the redeemer's diligence, the patient waiting on his sure action, the truth that Naomi knew Boaz would not rest till the matter was finished, Philippians 1:6, the figure of the redeemer who will not rest till the redemption is finished, the assurance of Naomi, the waiting on the redeemer, vv.13); Naomi bidding Ruth wait the issue (the patience, Philippians 1:6) — the waiting (sit still); 'the man will not be in rest, until he have finished the thing this day' (Boaz's diligence to finish the matter, the sure action, the figure, the redeemer who will not rest till the redemption is finished, the providence, the assurance, Boaz going up to the gate to follow, ch.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Then said she, Sit still, my daughter, until thou know how the matter will fall: for the man will not be in rest, until he have finished the thing this day.
Naomi bidding Ruth wait, for Boaz would not rest till the matter was finished
Naomi assuring Ruth that Boaz would settle the matter that very day
the redeemer who will not rest till the redemption is finished (Philippians 1:6)
